/.title: Neural Network
/.content:
Hello, and welcome to my neural network! This is where I store the notes I've written and compiled from my Mathematics and Computer Science degree at [UQ](https://uq.edu.au/).

The neural network functions very similarly to [Obsidian](https://obsidian.md/) and includes features like page backlinks, tags, and interactive graphs. You can also full-text search around the neural network with `Ctrl + k`.

## All Notes
You can view all the notes in the neural network in [list form](/notes) or in graph form below.

/notes/MATH1051/continuity.title: Continuity
/notes/MATH1051/continuity.content:
A [function](notes/MATH1051/functions.md) $f$ is *continuous* at a point $a \in \mathbb{R}$ if the [limit](notes/MATH1051/limits.md) of $f$ at $x = a$ exists and is equal to the function value at $a$, $f(a)$.

If $f$ is continuous at all values of $a \in \mathbb{R}$, $f$ is said to be continuous. If $f$ is not continuous at $a$, it is said to be *discontinuous*.

/notes/MATH1051/eulers-formula.title: Euler's Formula
/notes/MATH1051/eulers-formula.content:
*Euler's formula* states that for any real number $x$,

$$e^{ix} = \cos x + i \sin x.$$

When $x = \pi$, Euler's formula can be written in the form $e^{\pi i} + 1 = 0$, which is known as *Euler's identity*.

/notes/MATH1051/function-composition.title: Function Composition
/notes/MATH1051/function-composition.content:
Let $f: X \to Y$ and $g: Y \to Z$ be two [functions](notes/MATH1051/functions.md). The *composition* of $f$ and $g$, denoted $f \circ g: X \to Z$, is defined by

$$(f \circ g)(x) = f(g(x)).$$

Function composition is generally non-commutative; that is, $f \circ g \neq g \circ f$.

/notes/MATH1051/functions.title: Functions
/notes/MATH1051/functions.content:
A *function* $f$ from a set $X$ to a set $Y$, denoted $f: X \to Y$, assigns each element $x \in X$ exactly one element $f(x) \in Y$. The set $X$ is the *domain* of $f$ and the set $Y$ is the *codomain* of $f$.

The *image* or *range* of $f$, that is $f(X)$, is a subset of $Y$. The range is not necessarily equal to the codomain.

Geometrically, a function satisfies the vertical line test (any constant vertical line cuts through the function at most once).

/notes/MATH1051/injective-functions.title: Injective Function
/notes/MATH1051/injective-functions.content:
A [functions](notes/MATH1051/functions.md)$f: X \to Y$ is said to be *injective* or *one-to-one* if, for all $x_1, x_2 \in X$, we have $f(x_1) = f(x_2) \implies x_1 = x_2$.

Geometrically, a function is injective if it satisfies the horizontal line test (any constant horizontal line cuts through the function at most once).

/notes/MATH1051/intermediate-value-theor.title: Intermediate Value Theorem
/notes/MATH1051/intermediate-value-theor.content:
Let $f$ be a [continuous function](notes/MATH1051/continuity.md) defined on $[a, b]$, and $c$ some number such that $f(a) < c < f(b)$. Then there exists some $x$ in the domain $[a, b]$ such that $f(x) = c$.

A useful corollary of this theorem is that if a continuous function takes on negative and positive values inside an interval, it must also have a root (i.e. some $x$ in the domain such that $f(x) = 0$).

> 💭 Intuitively, a continuous path between two endpoint values must take on every intermediary value between those two values.

/notes/MATH1051/inverse-functions.title: Inverse Functions
/notes/MATH1051/inverse-functions.content: Let $f: X \to Y$ be a bijective ([injective function](notes/MATH1051/injective-functions.md) and surjective) function. The *inverse function* $f^{-1}: Y \to X$ is defined by $f^{-1}(y) = x$ for each $y \in Y$ and $x \in X$. We have $f^{-1}(y) = x \iff y = f(x)$.

/notes/MATH1051/limits.title: Limits
/notes/MATH1051/limits.content:
## Limit of a Sequence
Let $\{a_n\}_{n=0}^\infty$ be a [sequence](notes/MATH1051/sequences.md). If $a_n$ approaches some number $\mathscr{l} \in \mathbb{R}$ as $n$ gets larger and larger, we write

$$\lim_{n\to\infty} a_n = \mathscr{l}.$$

If a sequence $\{a_n\}$ has limit $\mathscr{l} \in \mathbb{R}$, we say that $a_n$ *converges* to $\mathscr{l}$, and that the sequence is *convergent*. Otherwise, $a_n$ *diverges* and the sequence is *divergent*.

## Limit of a Function

Let $f(x)$ be a [function](notes/MATH1051/functions.md) and $\mathscr{l} \in \mathbb{R}$. We say $f(x)$ *converges to* $\mathscr{l}$ or $f(x)$ *approaches the limit* $\mathscr{l}$ as $x$ approaches $a$ or $x$ tends to $a$, if we can get $f(x)$ to be arbitrarily close to $\mathscr{l}$ by taking $x$ arbitrarily close but not equal to $a$. We write

$$\lim_{x \to a}f(x) = \mathscr{l}.$$

### Infinite Limits

Let $f$ be a function that diverges, that is its value grows without bound. In this case we can define

$$\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = \infty$$

if $f$ diverges in the positive direction as we take $x$ arbitrarily and sufficiently close to $a$, and

$$\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = -\infty$$

if $f$ diverges in the negative direction as we take $x$ arbitrarily and sufficiently close to $a$. We also say that the limit *does not exist*.

/notes/MATH1051/sequences.title: Sequences
/notes/MATH1051/sequences.content: A *sequence* $\{a_n\}$ is an ordered list of numbers. Formally, a sequence can be defined as a [[notes/MATH1051/functions|function]] from the natural numbers $\mathbb{N}$ to the elements at each point in the sequence.

/notes/MATH1051/squeeze-theorem.title: Squeeze Theorem
/notes/MATH1051/squeeze-theorem.content:
## Squeeze Theorem (sequences)
Let $a_n, b_n, c_n$ be [sequences](notes/MATH1051/sequences.md) such that, for some $N \in \mathbb{N}$, we have $a_n \leq b_n \leq c_n$ for all $n \geq N$ and $\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} a_n = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} c_n = \mathscr{l}$. Then

$$\lim_{n\to\infty} b_n = \lim_{n\to\infty} a_n = \lim_{n\to\infty} b_n = \mathscr{l}.$$

## Squeeze Theorem (functions)
Let $f$, $g$, and $h$ be [functions](notes/MATH1051/functions.md) such that, for $a \in \mathbb{R}$, we have $h(x) \leq f(x) \leq g(x)$ for $x$ close to $a$ (i.e. $f$ is bounded by $h$ and $g$ around $x = a$) and $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to a} g(x) = \displaystyle\lim_{x \to a} h(x) = \mathscr{l}$. Then

$$\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = \mathscr{l}.$$
